# Cover Letter — Sno-Isle Libraries Data Strategist

---

My family uses Sno-Isle. My wife checks out novels. My toddler goes through picture books faster than we can return them, which is why I built a command-line tool integrating the BiblioCommons API with a rating and checkout-count system so I could queue holds before we even leave the house. I use the library myself for research — I am developing a precision agriculture company and the library's access to journals, databases, and community resources is part of how I do that work. Sno-Isle is not an abstract institution to me. It is infrastructure I depend on.

That is also why I take seriously what libraries provide for people who have fewer options than I do. Not everyone has an internet connection at home. For someone without a job who cannot afford one, the library is often the only place to search for work, submit applications, or access the digital systems that the rest of us take for granted. The library does not ask whether you can afford access. That commitment — to universal, unconditional access to information and the tools to use it — is not a feature. It is the entire point.

I encountered this firsthand in a way that shaped one of the projects I am most proud of. I met members of the Sno-Isle area transit advocacy community at a library branch. Those conversations led me to build a transit accessibility and vulnerability analysis for Snohomish County — integrating Census demographic data with GTFS transit feeds to map where service gaps overlapped with the populations least able to absorb them: elderly residents, car-free households, people with limited English proficiency, low-income families. The library was the community connection that made that work happen. That is what community infrastructure does.

My approach to data strategy comes down to something straightforward: the better a problem is understood, the more effectively it can be solved. At Randy's World-Wide, that meant forensically reconstructing what a predecessor had done with a broken accounting system before I could systematically fix it. In transit vulnerability work, it meant asking not just where the gaps were but who was living inside them. At Sno-Isle, it would mean asking honestly who is being reached by library services, who is not, and what the data can tell us about closing that distance.

I want to do this work here because I already live it as a patron. I know the geography, I know something of the population, and I believe in what this institution does in a way that is not separate from my technical skills — it is the reason I want to apply them here.

Simon-Hans Edasi
